from pathlib import Path


VULN_CLASSIFICATION_ROWS = "| web-prod | SQL Injection | OWASP A03 | Data extraction from database |"
SEVERITY_ROWS = "| VULN-001 | web-prod | SQL Injection | High | Data extraction from database |"
MITIGATION_ROWS = "| SQL Injection | Use parameterized queries, validate input, enforce least privilege |"
EXEC_SUMMARY = "The assessment identified a high-severity SQL Injection issue affecting web-prod, with evidence of database data extraction."
SYSTEM = "web-prod"
SCAN_SOURCE = "VibeSecSys v2.0 test data"
RISK_ROWS = "| web-prod | SQL Injection data extraction | Confidentiality loss and database compromise | High | High |"
CONCLUSION = "The identified SQL Injection vulnerability should be remediated as a priority through parameterized queries, input validation, and least-privilege database controls."


def read_template(path):
    return path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")


def replace_placeholders(content, replacements):
    for placeholder, value in replacements.items():
        content = content.replace(placeholder, value)
    return content


def main():
    base_dir = Path(__file__).resolve().parent
    templates_dir = base_dir.parent / "templates"

    vuln_section = replace_placeholders(
        read_template(templates_dir / "vuln_classification.txt"),
        {"{VULN_CLASSIFICATION_ROWS}": VULN_CLASSIFICATION_ROWS},
    )
    severity_section = replace_placeholders(
        read_template(templates_dir / "severity_assessment.txt"),
        {"{SEVERITY_ROWS}": SEVERITY_ROWS},
    )
    mitigation_section = replace_placeholders(
        read_template(templates_dir / "mitigation_table.txt"),
        {"{MITIGATION_ROWS}": MITIGATION_ROWS},
    )

    report_content = replace_placeholders(
        read_template(templates_dir / "full_report.txt"),
        {
            "{EXEC_SUMMARY}": EXEC_SUMMARY,
            "{SYSTEM}": SYSTEM,
            "{SCAN_SOURCE}": SCAN_SOURCE,
            "{VULN_CLASSIFICATION_SECTION}": vuln_section.strip(),
            "{SEVERITY_SECTION}": severity_section.strip(),
            "{RISK_ROWS}": RISK_ROWS,
            "{MITIGATION_SECTION}": mitigation_section.strip(),
            "{CONCLUSION}": CONCLUSION,
        },
    )

    output_path = base_dir / "final_report.md"
    output_path.write_text(report_content, encoding="utf-8")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

-MITIGATION_ROWS = "| SQL Injection | Use parameterized queries, validate input, enforce least privilege |"
+VULN_CLASSIFICATION_ROWS = """| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | OWASP A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components | FTP service on port 21 exposes vsftpd 2.3.4, creating risk of remote compromise and credential exposure |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Telnet Service | OWASP A02 Cryptographic Failures | Telnet service on port 23 may transmit credentials in cleartext |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | OWASP A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components | OpenSSH 4.7p1 on port 22 indicates outdated remote administration exposure |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | OWASP A05 Security Misconfiguration | Apache 2.2.8 on port 80, AJP13 on port 8009, and Tomcat/Coyote on port 8180 expose web and middleware attack surface |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Service Misconfiguration | Samba services on ports 139 and 445 may enable enumeration, file-share abuse, and lateral movement |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed r-services | Service Misconfiguration | exec and login services on ports 512 and 513 expose insecure remote command and login pathways |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Service Misconfiguration | Bash bindshell on port 1524 may allow direct shell access and full host compromise |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Service Misconfiguration | rpcbind on port 111 and NFS on port 2049 may expose file shares and support lateral movement |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Database Services | Service Misconfiguration | MySQL 5.0.51a on port 3306 and PostgreSQL 8.3.x on port 5432 expose database access to the network |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Service Misconfiguration | VNC on port 5900 and X11 on port 6000 expose remote graphical access surfaces |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | OWASP A05 Security Misconfiguration | Java RMI on port 1099 and UnrealIRCd on port 6667 expose remote service interfaces that may be abused |
+| ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Service Misconfiguration | OpenSSH 9.6p1 on port 22 and Apache 2.4.58 on port 80 expose management and web services requiring hardening |
+| ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Service Misconfiguration | MSRPC on port 135, NetBIOS on port 139, and Microsoft-DS on port 445 expose Windows file-sharing and remote procedure services |"""
+SEVERITY_ROWS = """| VULN-001 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Critical | Remote compromise risk and credential exposure from vulnerable FTP service | MIT-001 |
+| VULN-002 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Telnet Service | High | Credential interception and unauthorized remote access | MIT-002 |
+| VULN-003 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Medium | Remote administration exposure with outdated service version | MIT-003 |
+| VULN-004 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | High | Web application compromise, middleware abuse, and backend exposure | MIT-004 |
+| VULN-005 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | High | File-share enumeration, unauthorized access, and lateral movement | MIT-005 |
+| VULN-006 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed r-services | Critical | Unauthorized remote command execution and insecure login access | MIT-006 |
+| VULN-007 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Critical | Direct shell access, full host compromise, and privilege escalation | MIT-007 |
+| VULN-008 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | High | Unauthorized file access and lateral movement through exposed file-sharing services | MIT-008 |
+| VULN-009 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Database Services | High | Unauthorized database access and data exfiltration | MIT-009 |
+| VULN-010 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | High | Remote session compromise and unauthorized system interaction | MIT-010 |
+| VULN-011 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | High | Remote service abuse and potential exploitation of exposed interfaces | MIT-011 |
+| VULN-012 | ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Medium | Brute-force attempts, web exposure, and service-specific exploitation | MIT-012 |
+| VULN-013 | ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | High | SMB enumeration, credential relay, lateral movement, and unauthorized file-sharing access | MIT-013 |"""
+MITIGATION_ROWS = """| MIT-001 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Disable vulnerable FTP service or replace it with SFTP/FTPS and upgrade to a supported secure version |
+| MIT-002 | Exposed Telnet Service | Disable Telnet and use SSH with strong authentication and restricted network access |
+| MIT-003 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Upgrade OpenSSH, disable weak algorithms, enforce key-based authentication, and restrict administrative access |
+| MIT-004 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | Patch Apache and Tomcat components, restrict AJP to trusted internal hosts, and disable unused web services |
+| MIT-005 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Restrict SMB and NetBIOS to trusted networks, disable anonymous access, and harden share permissions |
+| MIT-006 | Exposed r-services | Disable exec and login services and replace them with hardened SSH-based administration |
+| MIT-007 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Remove the bindshell immediately, isolate the host, and investigate for compromise |
+| MIT-008 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Restrict NFS and rpcbind to approved hosts, enforce least-privilege exports, and block unnecessary access |
+| MIT-009 | Exposed Database Services | Bind databases to trusted interfaces only, enforce strong authentication, patch database versions, and apply least privilege |
+| MIT-010 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Restrict VNC and X11 to trusted networks or encrypted tunnels and enforce strong authentication |
+| MIT-011 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | Restrict Java RMI and IRC services, patch or remove unused components, and monitor for abuse |
+| MIT-012 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Harden SSH and HTTP services with patching, secure configuration, access control, and monitoring |
+| MIT-013 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Restrict RPC and SMB to trusted networks, enable SMB signing, patch Windows services, and disable unnecessary file-sharing exposure |"""
*** End Patch

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

payload.message:
My understanding of `./V2.0/experiments/SYSTEM_script.txt`:

This is a focused v2.0 prompt for generating only the **SYSTEM section** of a cybersecurity report.

**Input**

The prompt says to read:

```text
../Samples/VibeSecSys.xml
```

Given the v2.0 structure, the intended file is likely:

```text
V2.0/samples/VibeSecSys.xml
```

There may be a case mismatch: `Samples` vs `samples`. On Windows this likely works, but for consistency the actual folder is:

```text
V2.0/samples/
```

**Task**

Derive the SYSTEM section for a report.

This section should describe the assessed environment, not the vulnerabilities.

**What To Include**

The SYSTEM section should identify:

- All assessed hosts by IP address
- The number of systems assessed
- A high-level description of the environment
- Types of systems inferred from exposed services

For example, based on previously read XML data, likely systems include:

```text
192.168.56.20
192.168.56.30
192.168.56.50
```

The environment may be described as including:

- A multi-service Linux/server host
- A web-facing host
- A Windows-style host exposing RPC/SMB services

**What Not To Include**

The prompt explicitly says:

- Do not reference vulnerabilities yet.
- Do not explain how the information was derived.
- Do not include process explanation.
- Do not include anything outside the SYSTEM section text.

So this should not mention things like:

- “Telnet is insecure”
- “SMB may allow lateral movement”
- “Critical risk”
- “Misconfiguration”
- “I parsed the XML”

**Tone**

Use:

```text
professional academic tone
```

So the section should be formal, concise, and suitable for an academic cybersecurity report.

**Expected Output**

When executed, the response should return only the SYSTEM section text.

No headings unless you want the section to be literally titled `SYSTEM`, but the prompt says “section text,” so this is a small point to clarify. My safest interpretation is to provide a clean report-ready paragraph or short section, not a table and not bullet-heavy output.

payload.arguments: {"command":"& { [xml]$xml = Get-Content -LiteralPath '.\\V2.0\\samples\\VibeSecSys.xml'; foreach ($hostNode in $xml.nmaprun.host) { $addr = ($hostNode.address | Where-Object { $_.addrtype -eq 'ipv4' } | Select-Object -First 1).addr; $hostname = ($hostNode.hostnames.hostname | Select-Object -First 1).name; $services = @(); foreach ($port in $hostNode.ports.port) { if ($port.state.state -eq 'open') { $services += $port.service.name } }; [PSCustomObject]@{Host=$addr; Hostname=$hostname; OpenServices=($services -join ', ')} } } | Format-List","workdir":"d:\\AJAY\\MSc CyberSecurity\\Modules\\OneDrive - Kingston University\\NIS\\CI7530 - CyberSecurity and Artificial Intelligence - 30\\CourseWork2\\VibeSecSys","timeout_ms":10000}

payload.message:
My understanding of `./V2.0/experiments/RISK_ROWS_script.txt`:

This prompt is asking to generate only the `RISK_ROWS` content for the report template.

**Purpose**

The goal is to create rows for the OCTAVE/STRIDE-style risk assessment table in the v2.0 full report.

This table summarises risk at the asset/threat level, using the asset IDs and vulnerability/severity findings already produced in previous steps.

**Input Context**

Use:

```text
Assets derived from ../Samples/VibeSecSys.xml
```

In the actual project, this maps to:

```text
V2.0/samples/VibeSecSys.xml
```

Also use the previously identified vulnerability/severity information.

Known assets from previous steps are:

```text
ASSET-001 -> 192.168.56.20
ASSET-002 -> 192.168.56.30
ASSET-003 -> 192.168.56.50
```

**Required Format**

Each row must follow:

```text
| Asset ID | Threat | Impact | Likelihood | Risk Level |
```

**Expected Content**

One or more rows per asset.

For example:

- `ASSET-001` may need several rows because it has many exposed high-risk services:
  - legacy protocol exposure
  - bindshell/r-services exposure
  - database exposure
  - file-sharing/middleware exposure

- `ASSET-002` likely needs fewer rows:
  - SSH and HTTP service exposure

- `ASSET-003` likely needs rows for:
  - Windows RPC/SMB/NetBIOS exposure

**Risk Logic**

Risk should reflect:

- Severity
- Exposure level
- Type of service
- Potential impact

So likely:

- `ASSET-001`: Critical/High risk because of bindshell, r-services, FTP/Telnet, databases, NFS, VNC/X11, Java RMI/AJP
- `ASSET-002`: Medium risk because SSH/HTTP exposure is narrower
- `ASSET-003`: High risk because SMB/RPC exposure can support enumeration, credential relay, and lateral movement

**Output Rule**

When executed, return only:

```text
RISK_ROWS:
<rows>
```

or possibly just the rows themselves.

The prompt says:

```text
Return ONLY RISK_ROWS.
```

So I would include the block label:

```text
RISK_ROWS:
```

followed by table rows, unless you prefer rows without the label.

No explanations, no extra text.

My understanding of `./V2.0/experiments/EXEC_SUMMARY_script.txt`:

This prompt is asking to generate only the **EXEC_SUMMARY** text for the v2.0 report.

**Purpose**

The Executive Summary should provide a high-level overview of the assessment results for non-technical or management readers.

It should summarise the overall risk posture based on the previously generated system description, severity distribution, and risk rows.

**Inputs To Use**

Use information already derived in previous v2.0 steps:

1. SYSTEM description

Known environment:

- 3 assessed hosts
- `192.168.56.20`
- `192.168.56.30`
- `192.168.56.50`
- Mixed environment with Linux/Unix-style services, web service exposure, and Windows RPC/SMB services

2. Severity distribution

Previously identified findings included:

- Critical findings on `ASSET-001`
- High findings on `ASSET-001` and `ASSET-003`
- Medium findings on `ASSET-001` and `ASSET-002`

3. Risk assessment results

Known risk posture:

- `ASSET-001` is the highest-risk asset due to legacy, vulnerable, remote access, database, file-sharing, and shell-like services
- `ASSET-002` has a more limited Medium exposure profile
- `ASSET-003` has High risk from Windows RPC/SMB exposure

**Tone**

The prompt specifically asks for:

```text
High-level, non-technical tone
```

So the summary should avoid too much port/service detail. It should speak in business/security terms such as:

- elevated risk
- broad exposure
- urgent remediation
- unauthorized access risk
- data exposure
- operational disruption
- prioritised hardening

**Must Reflect Actual Findings**

It should not be generic. It should mention real findings from this project, such as:

- three assessed systems
- one high-risk host with multiple critical/high findings
- one moderate web/SSH host
- one Windows services host with file-sharing exposure
- overall risk posture is elevated/high due to critical and high exposure

**Output Rule**

When executed, return only the executive summary text.

No heading, no explanation, no bullet labels unless the summary itself needs them. A concise paragraph is likely best.

My understanding of `./V2.0/experiments/CONCLUSION_script.txt`:

This prompt is asking to generate only the **CONCLUSION** text for the v2.0 cybersecurity analysis report.

**Purpose**

The conclusion should close the report by summarising the overall security posture and the main remediation priorities.

It should be report-ready and suitable for the final section of the generated cybersecurity report.

**Inputs To Use**

Use context already derived from the same XML scan data:

- SYSTEM section
- EXEC_SUMMARY
- RISK_ROWS
- Vulnerability severity findings
- Mitigation themes

So the conclusion should align with previous findings:

- Three assessed systems
- Highest risk on `192.168.56.20`
- Critical/high risks from legacy services, exposed remote access, shell-like services, databases, file sharing, and middleware
- Moderate exposure on `192.168.56.30`
- High SMB/RPC-related exposure on `192.168.56.50`
- Remediation themes around service reduction, access restriction, patching, authentication hardening, and monitoring

**What The Conclusion Should Do**

It should:

- Summarise the overall security posture
- Emphasise priority remediation areas
- Stay concise
- Use professional academic tone
- Be suitable for direct insertion into the report

**What It Must Not Do**

It must not:

- Introduce new findings
- Repeat the executive summary word-for-word
- Use bullet points
- Include a heading
- Include explanation about how it was generated

**Output Rule**

When executed, return only the conclusion text as a paragraph or short set of paragraphs.

No heading, no bullets, no extra commentary.

The assessed environment demonstrates a security posture that requires prioritised remediation, particularly around the concentration of critical and high-risk exposures on 192.168.56.20. The presence of legacy protocols, exposed administrative services, database access, file-sharing services, and shell-like access pathways substantially increases the likelihood of unauthorised access, credential compromise, lateral movement, and service abuse. Remediation should therefore focus first on removing unnecessary and high-risk services, restricting access to trusted networks, strengthening authentication, patching outdated components, and improving monitoring. Addressing these areas will reduce the attack surface and improve the resilience of the assessed environment.

payload.message:
I re-read the updated `./V2.0/experiments/FINAL_REPORT_MD.txt`.

The path issue is now resolved. The prompt now correctly uses `V2.0`.

**Purpose**

This is the final v2.0 Markdown report assembly step.

It is not asking for new analysis or code. It is asking to assemble the final report by inserting already validated content into the master template.

**Template To Use**

Read the master report template:

```text
./V2.0/templates/full_report.txt
```

This file defines the final report structure.

**Content To Insert**

Use the final validated content derived from the same XML scan for these sections/placeholders:

```text
SYSTEM
SCAN_SOURCE
VULN_CLASSIFICATION_SECTION
SEVERITY_SECTION
RISK_ROWS
MITIGATION_SECTION
EXEC_SUMMARY
CONCLUSION
```

These correspond to placeholders in the master template, such as:

```text
{SYSTEM}
{SCAN_SOURCE}
{VULN_CLASSIFICATION_SECTION}
{SEVERITY_SECTION}
{RISK_ROWS}
{MITIGATION_SECTION}
{EXEC_SUMMARY}
{CONCLUSION}
```

**Important Requirement**

Write the final assembled Markdown report to disk at:

```text
./V2.0/experiments/final_report.md
```

So this step will overwrite or replace the current `final_report.md` in the experiments folder.

**Strict Rules**

The final report must:

- Follow the structure of `full_report.txt` exactly
- Not change the wording of the provided validated content
- Not add new findings
- Not explain anything
- Not generate code
- Output only the final report text

**Execution Understanding**

When approved, I should:

1. Read `V2.0/templates/full_report.txt`.
2. Use the validated content from the previous v2.0 steps:
   - SYSTEM text
   - SCAN_SOURCE text
   - final vulnerability classification section
   - final severity section
   - final risk rows
   - final mitigation section
   - executive summary
   - conclusion
3. Replace the matching placeholders in the template.
4. Save the final Markdown report to:

```text
V2.0/experiments/final_report.md
```

5. Return only the final report text.

**Clarification Point**

The prompt still contains placeholders like:

```text
<PASTE FINAL SYSTEM TEXT HERE>
```

So the actual final content needs to come from the outputs we already generated in previous steps. My understanding is that I should use the validated text and row blocks from this conversation rather than leaving those placeholders unresolved.

This is a final v2.0 report assembly step.

```text
./V2.0/templates/full_report.txt
```

This template controls the final report structure.

Write the assembled Markdown report to:

```text
./V2.0/experiments/final_report_NEW.md
```

Do not print the report to the terminal.

```text
SYSTEM
SCAN_SOURCE
VULN_CLASSIFICATION_SECTION
SEVERITY_SECTION
RISK_ROWS

CONCLUSION
```

```text

{VULN_CLASSIFICATION_SECTION}
{SEVERITY_SECTION}
{RISK_ROWS}
{MITIGATION_SECTION}
{EXEC_SUMMARY}
```

Because the prompt says:


the cleanest version would be for `FINAL_REPORT_MD1.txt` to contain the actual content directly. Then I can insert it exactly into the template without interpretation.

*** Add File: d:\AJAY\MSc CyberSecurity\Modules\OneDrive - Kingston University\NIS\CI7530 - CyberSecurity and Artificial Intelligence - 30\CourseWork2\VibeSecSys\V2.0\experiments\final_report_NEW.md
+# Cybersecurity Analysis Report
+
+---
+
+## 1. Executive Summary
+The assessment identified an elevated overall risk posture across three systems, with the greatest concern concentrated on 192.168.56.20 due to multiple critical and high-risk exposures involving legacy services, remote access pathways, database access, file-sharing services, and shell-like access. The environment also includes a smaller web-facing system at 192.168.56.30 with moderate exposure and a Windows-oriented host at 192.168.56.50 with high-risk RPC and SMB-related exposure. Priority remediation should focus on reducing unnecessary services, removing critical remote access risks, restricting exposed services to trusted networks, and hardening authentication and monitoring controls.
+
+---
+
+## 2. System Overview
+- Target System: The assessment scope consists of three systems within the 192.168.56.0/24 network segment: 192.168.56.20, 192.168.56.30, and 192.168.56.50. The host at 192.168.56.20 is identified as filesrv01 and presents a broad multi-service server profile, including remote access, file-sharing, database, web, naming, and administrative service functions. The host at 192.168.56.30 is identified as web-prod and presents a focused service profile consistent with a web-facing system offering SSH and HTTP services. The host at 192.168.56.50 presents characteristics of a Windows-based system, with Microsoft RPC, NetBIOS, and Microsoft-DS services exposed. Overall, the assessed environment represents a mixed service landscape containing Linux/Unix-style server functions, a web production host, and a Windows-oriented network service host.
+- Scan Source: The scan source is an Nmap 7.92 XML output file generated using service/version detection and default script scanning. The scan data was collected in XML format from the targets filesrv01, web-prod, and 192.168.56.50, then processed to identify assessed hosts, open ports, exposed services, service metadata, and related security observations for reporting.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Vulnerability Classification
+
+### Vulnerability Classification Table
+
+| Asset ID | System | Vulnerability | Classification | Evidence-Based Impact |
+|--------|--------------|----------------|----------------------|
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | OWASP A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components | FTP service on port 21 exposes vsftpd 2.3.4, creating risk of remote compromise and credential exposure |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Telnet Service | OWASP A02 Cryptographic Failures | Telnet service on port 23 may transmit credentials in cleartext |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | OWASP A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components | OpenSSH 4.7p1 on port 22 indicates outdated remote administration exposure |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | OWASP A05 Security Misconfiguration | Apache 2.2.8 on port 80, AJP13 on port 8009, and Tomcat/Coyote on port 8180 expose web and middleware attack surface |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Service Misconfiguration | Samba services on ports 139 and 445 may enable enumeration, file-share abuse, and lateral movement |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed r-services | Service Misconfiguration | exec and login services on ports 512 and 513 expose insecure remote command and login pathways |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Service Misconfiguration | Bash bindshell on port 1524 may allow direct shell access and full host compromise |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Service Misconfiguration | rpcbind on port 111 and NFS on port 2049 may expose file shares and support lateral movement |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Database Services | Service Misconfiguration | MySQL 5.0.51a on port 3306 and PostgreSQL 8.3.x on port 5432 expose database access to the network |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Service Misconfiguration | VNC on port 5900 and X11 on port 6000 expose remote graphical access surfaces |
+| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | OWASP A05 Security Misconfiguration | Java RMI on port 1099 and UnrealIRCd on port 6667 expose remote service interfaces that may be abused |
+| ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Service Misconfiguration | OpenSSH 9.6p1 on port 22 and Apache 2.4.58 on port 80 expose management and web services requiring hardening |
+| ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Service Misconfiguration | MSRPC on port 135, NetBIOS on port 139, and Microsoft-DS on port 445 expose Windows file-sharing and remote procedure services |
+
+---
+
+## 4. Severity Assessment
+
+### Vulnerability Severity and Impact Assessment
+
+| VULN ID | Asset ID | Target System | Vulnerability | Severity | Potential Impact | Mitigation ID |
+|----|--------------|--------------|----------|------------------|
+| VULN-001 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Critical | Remote compromise risk and credential exposure from vulnerable FTP service | MIT-001 |
+| VULN-002 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Telnet Service | High | Credential interception and unauthorized remote access | MIT-002 |
+| VULN-003 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Medium | Remote administration exposure with outdated service version | MIT-003 |
+| VULN-004 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | High | Web application compromise, middleware abuse, and backend exposure | MIT-004 |
+| VULN-005 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | High | File-share enumeration, unauthorized access, and lateral movement | MIT-005 |
+| VULN-006 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed r-services | Critical | Unauthorized remote command execution and insecure login access | MIT-006 |
+| VULN-007 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Critical | Direct shell access, full host compromise, and privilege escalation | MIT-007 |
+| VULN-008 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | High | Unauthorized file access and lateral movement through exposed file-sharing services | MIT-008 |
+| VULN-009 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Database Services | High | Unauthorized database access and data exfiltration | MIT-009 |
+| VULN-010 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | High | Remote session compromise and unauthorized system interaction | MIT-010 |
+| VULN-011 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | High | Remote service abuse and potential exploitation of exposed interfaces | MIT-011 |
+| VULN-012 | ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Medium | Brute-force attempts, web exposure, and service-specific exploitation | MIT-012 |
+| VULN-013 | ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | High | SMB enumeration, credential relay, lateral movement, and unauthorized file-sharing access | MIT-013 |
+
+---
+
+## 5. Risk Assessment (OCTAVE / STRIDE)
+
+| Asset | Threat | Impact | Likelihood | Risk Level |
+|------|--------|--------|------------|------------|
+| ASSET-001 | Legacy and vulnerable service exposure | Credential theft, unauthorized remote access, lateral movement, and host compromise | High | Critical |
+| ASSET-001 | Exposed database services | Unauthorized database access, data exfiltration, and integrity compromise | High | High |
+| ASSET-001 | Exposed file-sharing and remote administration services | Unauthorized file access, remote command execution, and privilege escalation | High | Critical |
+| ASSET-001 | Exposed web and middleware services | Web application compromise, backend service abuse, and remote exploitation | Medium | High |
+| ASSET-002 | Exposed SSH and HTTP services | Brute-force attempts, web service exploitation, and unauthorized access | Medium | Medium |
+| ASSET-003 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB services | SMB enumeration, credential relay, unauthorized file-sharing access, and lateral movement | High | High |
+
+---
+
+## 6. Mitigation Recommendations
+
+### Mitigation Recommendations
+
+| MIT ID | Vulnerability | Recommended Mitigation |
+|---------------|----------------------|
+| MIT-001 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Disable vulnerable FTP service or replace it with SFTP/FTPS and upgrade to a supported secure version |
+| MIT-002 | Exposed Telnet Service | Disable Telnet and use SSH with strong authentication and restricted network access |
+| MIT-003 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Upgrade OpenSSH, disable weak algorithms, enforce key-based authentication, and restrict administrative access |
+| MIT-004 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | Patch Apache and Tomcat components, restrict AJP to trusted internal hosts, and disable unused web services |
+| MIT-005 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Restrict SMB and NetBIOS to trusted networks, disable anonymous access, and harden share permissions |
+| MIT-006 | Exposed r-services | Disable exec and login services and replace them with hardened SSH-based administration |
+| MIT-007 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Remove the bindshell immediately, isolate the host, and investigate for compromise |
+| MIT-008 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Restrict NFS and rpcbind to approved hosts, enforce least-privilege exports, and block unnecessary access |
+| MIT-009 | Exposed Database Services | Bind databases to trusted interfaces only, enforce strong authentication, patch database versions, and apply least privilege |
+| MIT-010 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Restrict VNC and X11 to trusted networks or encrypted tunnels and enforce strong authentication |
+| MIT-011 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | Restrict Java RMI and IRC services, patch or remove unused components, and monitor for abuse |
+| MIT-012 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Harden SSH and HTTP services with patching, secure configuration, access control, and monitoring |
+| MIT-013 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Restrict RPC and SMB to trusted networks, enable SMB signing, patch Windows services, and disable unnecessary file-sharing exposure |
+
+---
+
+## 7. Conclusion
+
+The assessed environment demonstrates a security posture that requires prioritised remediation, particularly around the concentration of critical and high-risk exposures on 192.168.56.20. The presence of legacy protocols, exposed administrative services, database access, file-sharing services, and shell-like access pathways substantially increases the likelihood of unauthorised access, credential compromise, lateral movement, and service abuse. Remediation should therefore focus first on removing unnecessary and high-risk services, restricting access to trusted networks, strengthening authentication, patching outdated components, and improving monitoring. Addressing these areas will reduce the attack surface and improve the resilience of the assessed environment.
*** End Patch

The host at 192.168.56.30 is identified as web-prod and presents a focused service profile consistent with a web-facing system offering SSH and HTTP services. ## 4. Severity Assessment

| ASSET-003 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB services | SMB enumeration, credential relay, unauthorized file-sharing access, and lateral movement | High | High |

## 6. Mitigation Recommendations

### Mitigation Recommendations

| MIT ID | Vulnerability | Recommended Mitigation |
|---------------|----------------------|
| MIT-001 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Disable vulnerable FTP service or replace it with SFTP/FTPS and upgrade to a supported secure version |
| MIT-002 | Exposed Telnet Service | Disable Telnet and use SSH with strong authentication and restricted network access |
| MIT-003 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Upgrade OpenSSH, disable weak algorithms, enforce key-based authentication, and restrict administrative access |

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

payload.message:
My understanding of `./V2.0/experiments/ReportRefinement.txt`:

This is a **report refinement step** for the already generated v2.0 final report.

**Target File**

The file to refine is:

```text
./V2.0/experiments/final_report_NEW.md
```

Changes should be saved back to the same file.

**Purpose**

The goal is only to fix table formatting issues in the report.

This is not a content-generation step. It should not create new findings, change wording, alter data, or reorder sections.

**Strict Rules**

When executed:

- Do not print the report.
- Do not generate new content.
- Do not explain anything during the operation.
- Only fix formatting and save the file.

**What Needs To Be Fixed**

All tables in the report should be valid Markdown tables.

Each table must have:

```markdown
| Column 1 | Column 2 |
|---|---|
| value | value |
```

Specifically:

- Use `|` column separators.
- Include a header row.
- Include a separator row.
- Ensure each data row starts and ends with `|`.

**Tables To Check/Fix**

The prompt names four tables:

1. Vulnerability Classification Table
2. Severity Assessment Table
3. Risk Assessment Table
4. Mitigation Recommendations Table

**HTML Cleanup**

If any HTML table tags exist, remove/convert them:

```html
<table>
<tr>
<td>
<th>
```

These should not remain in the Markdown file.

**Malformed Row Cleanup**

Rows like:

```text
MIT-001 | VULN-001 | ASSET-001 ...
```

should be corrected to:

```markdown
| MIT-001 | VULN-001 | ASSET-001 ... |
```

**What Must Not Change**

Do not change:

- Wording
- Data values
- Section order
- Meaning
- Report structure beyond table formatting

**Expected Result**

After execution, `final_report_NEW.md` should remain the same report, but all tables should be clean, valid Markdown and easier to read/render.

payload.message:
I re-read the updated `./V2.0/experiments/ReportRefinement.txt`.

The new important addition is:

```text
Each section must contain exactly one complete, properly formatted Markdown table.
Do not split or fragment tables.
```

My understanding:

**Purpose**

This is still a **formatting-only refinement step** for the generated v2.0 report.

It should not create new analysis or change report meaning. It should only fix table formatting.

**Input File**

Read:

```text
./V2.0/experiments/final_report_NEW.md
```

This is the source report.

**Output File**

Save the refined version as:

```text
./V2.0/experiments/final_report_NEW_v2.md
```

So the original `final_report_NEW.md` should remain unchanged.

**Main Table Requirement**

Each affected section must contain exactly **one complete Markdown table**.

That applies to:

1. Vulnerability Classification Table
2. Severity Assessment Table
3. Risk Assessment Table
4. Mitigation Recommendations Table

So the output should not have broken tables, repeated table headers, partial fragments, or separated row groups within the same section.

**Markdown Table Requirements**

Each table must have:

```markdown
| Header 1 | Header 2 |
|---|---|
| Row value | Row value |
```

Specifically:

- Use `|` separators.
- Include a header row.
- Include a separator row.
- Ensure all rows start and end with `|`.
- Ensure rows have the right number of columns for that table.

**HTML Cleanup**

If any HTML table tags are present, remove or convert them:

```html
<table>
<tr>
<td>
<th>
```

**Malformed Row Fixes**

Rows like:

```text
MIT-001 | VULN-001 | ASSET-001 ...
```

must become proper Markdown rows:

```markdown
| MIT-001 | VULN-001 | ASSET-001 ... |
```

**What Must Not Change**

Do not change:

- wording
- data
- section order
- findings
- meaning

So this is not an opportunity to rewrite the report. It is only table cleanup.

**Execution Behavior**

When approved, I should:

1. Open `final_report_NEW.md`.
2. Inspect the four table sections.
3. Fix only Markdown table formatting.
4. Ensure each named section contains one complete table.
5. Save the refined file as `final_report_NEW_v2.md`.
6. Not print the report content to the terminal.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.aggregated_output:
# Cybersecurity Analysis Report

---

## 1. Executive Summary
The assessment identified an elevated overall risk posture across three systems, with the greatest concern concentrated on 192.168.56.20 due to multiple critical and high-risk exposures involving legacy services, remote access pathways, database access, file-sharing services, and shell-like access. The environment also includes a smaller web-facing system at 192.168.56.30 with moderate exposure and a Windows-oriented host at 192.168.56.50 with high-risk RPC and SMB-related exposure. Priority remediation should focus on reducing unnecessary services, removing critical remote access risks, restricting exposed services to trusted networks, and hardening authentication and monitoring controls.

---

## 2. System Overview
- Target System: The assessment scope consists of three systems within the 192.168.56.0/24 network segment: 192.168.56.20, 192.168.56.30, and 192.168.56.50. The host at 192.168.56.20 is identified as filesrv01 and presents a broad multi-service server profile, including remote access, file-sharing, database, web, naming, and administrative service functions. The host at 192.168.56.30 is identified as web-prod and presents a focused service profile consistent with a web-facing system offering SSH and HTTP services. The host at 192.168.56.50 presents characteristics of a Windows-based system, with Microsoft RPC, NetBIOS, and Microsoft-DS services exposed. Overall, the assessed environment represents a mixed service landscape containing Linux/Unix-style server functions, a web production host, and a Windows-oriented network service host.
- Scan Source: The scan source is an Nmap 7.92 XML output file generated using service/version detection and default script scanning. The scan data was collected in XML format from the targets filesrv01, web-prod, and 192.168.56.50, then processed to identify assessed hosts, open ports, exposed services, service metadata, and related security observations for reporting.

---

## 3. Vulnerability Classification

### Vulnerability Classification Table

| Asset ID | System | Vulnerability | Classification | Evidence-Based Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | OWASP A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components | FTP service on port 21 exposes vsftpd 2.3.4, creating risk of remote compromise and credential exposure |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Telnet Service | OWASP A02 Cryptographic Failures | Telnet service on port 23 may transmit credentials in cleartext |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | OWASP A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components | OpenSSH 4.7p1 on port 22 indicates outdated remote administration exposure |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | OWASP A05 Security Misconfiguration | Apache 2.2.8 on port 80, AJP13 on port 8009, and Tomcat/Coyote on port 8180 expose web and middleware attack surface |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Service Misconfiguration | Samba services on ports 139 and 445 may enable enumeration, file-share abuse, and lateral movement |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed r-services | Service Misconfiguration | exec and login services on ports 512 and 513 expose insecure remote command and login pathways |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Service Misconfiguration | Bash bindshell on port 1524 may allow direct shell access and full host compromise |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Service Misconfiguration | rpcbind on port 111 and NFS on port 2049 may expose file shares and support lateral movement |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Database Services | Service Misconfiguration | MySQL 5.0.51a on port 3306 and PostgreSQL 8.3.x on port 5432 expose database access to the network |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Service Misconfiguration | VNC on port 5900 and X11 on port 6000 expose remote graphical access surfaces |
| ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | OWASP A05 Security Misconfiguration | Java RMI on port 1099 and UnrealIRCd on port 6667 expose remote service interfaces that may be abused |
| ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Service Misconfiguration | OpenSSH 9.6p1 on port 22 and Apache 2.4.58 on port 80 expose management and web services requiring hardening |
| ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Service Misconfiguration | MSRPC on port 135, NetBIOS on port 139, and Microsoft-DS on port 445 expose Windows file-sharing and remote procedure services |

---

## 4. Severity Assessment

### Vulnerability Severity and Impact Assessment

| VULN ID | Asset ID | Target System | Vulnerability | Severity | Potential Impact | Mitigation ID |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| VULN-001 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Se…967 chars truncated…, full host compromise, and privilege escalation | MIT-007 |
| VULN-008 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | High | Unauthorized file access and lateral movement through exposed file-sharing services | MIT-008 |
| VULN-009 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Database Services | High | Unauthorized database access and data exfiltration | MIT-009 |
| VULN-010 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | High | Remote session compromise and unauthorized system interaction | MIT-010 |
| VULN-011 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | High | Remote service abuse and potential exploitation of exposed interfaces | MIT-011 |
| VULN-012 | ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Medium | Brute-force attempts, web exposure, and service-specific exploitation | MIT-012 |
| VULN-013 | ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | High | SMB enumeration, credential relay, lateral movement, and unauthorized file-sharing access | MIT-013 |

---

## 5. Risk Assessment (OCTAVE / STRIDE)

| Asset | Threat | Impact | Likelihood | Risk Level |
|------|--------|--------|------------|------------|
| ASSET-001 | Legacy and vulnerable service exposure | Credential theft, unauthorized remote access, lateral movement, and host compromise | High | Critical |
| ASSET-001 | Exposed database services | Unauthorized database access, data exfiltration, and integrity compromise | High | High |
| ASSET-001 | Exposed file-sharing and remote administration services | Unauthorized file access, remote command execution, and privilege escalation | High | Critical |
| ASSET-001 | Exposed web and middleware services | Web application compromise, backend service abuse, and remote exploitation | Medium | High |
| ASSET-002 | Exposed SSH and HTTP services | Brute-force attempts, web service exploitation, and unauthorized access | Medium | Medium |
| ASSET-003 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB services | SMB enumeration, credential relay, unauthorized file-sharing access, and lateral movement | High | High |

---

## 6. Mitigation Recommendations

### Mitigation Recommendations

| MIT ID | Vulnerability | Recommended Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| MIT-001 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Disable vulnerable FTP service or replace it with SFTP/FTPS and upgrade to a supported secure version |
| MIT-002 | Exposed Telnet Service | Disable Telnet and use SSH with strong authentication and restricted network access |
| MIT-003 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Upgrade OpenSSH, disable weak algorithms, enforce key-based authentication, and restrict administrative access |
| MIT-004 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | Patch Apache and Tomcat components, restrict AJP to trusted internal hosts, and disable unused web services |
| MIT-005 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Restrict SMB and NetBIOS to trusted networks, disable anonymous access, and harden share permissions |
| MIT-006 | Exposed r-services | Disable exec and login services and replace them with hardened SSH-based administration |
| MIT-007 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Remove the bindshell immediately, isolate the host, and investigate for compromise |
| MIT-008 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Restrict NFS and rpcbind to approved hosts, enforce least-privilege exports, and block unnecessary access |
| MIT-009 | Exposed Database Services | Bind databases to trusted interfaces only, enforce strong authentication, patch database versions, and apply least privilege |
| MIT-010 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Restrict VNC and X11 to trusted networks or encrypted tunnels and enforce strong authentication |
| MIT-011 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | Restrict Java RMI and IRC services, patch or remove unused components, and monitor for abuse |
| MIT-012 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Harden SSH and HTTP services with patching, secure configuration, access control, and monitoring |
| MIT-013 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Restrict RPC and SMB to trusted networks, enable SMB signing, patch Windows services, and disable unnecessary file-sharing exposure |

---

## 7. Conclusion

The assessed environment demonstrates a security posture that requires prioritised remediation, particularly around the concentration of critical and high-risk exposures on 192.168.56.20. The presence of legacy protocols, exposed administrative services, database access, file-sharing services, and shell-like access pathways substantially increases the likelihood of unauthorised access, credential compromise, lateral movement, and service abuse. Remediation should therefore focus first on removing unnecessary and high-risk services, restricting access to trusted networks, strengthening authentication, patching outdated components, and improving monitoring. Addressing these areas will reduce the attack surface and improve the resilience of the assessed environment.

## 2. System Overview

The host at 192.168.56.30 is identified as web-prod and presents a focused service profile consistent with a web-facing system offering SSH and HTTP services. The host at 192.168.56.50 presents characteristics of a Windows-based system, with Microsoft RPC, NetBIOS, and Microsoft-DS services exposed. Overall, the assessed environment represents a mixed service landscape containing Linux/Unix-style server functions, a web production host, and a Windows-oriented network service host.
- Scan Source: The scan source is an Nmap 7.92 XML output file generated using service/version detection and default script scanning. The scan data was collected in XML format from the targets filesrv01, web-prod, and 192.168.56.50, then processed to identify assessed hosts, open ports, exposed services, service metadata, and related security observations for reporting.

| VULN-011 | ASSET-001 | 192.168.56.20 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | High | Remote service abuse and potential exploitation of exposed interfaces | MIT-011 |
| VULN-012 | ASSET-002 | 192.168.56.30 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Medium | Brute-force attempts, web exposure, and service-specific exploitation | MIT-012 |
| VULN-013 | ASSET-003 | 192.168.56.50 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | High | SMB enumeration, credential relay, lateral movement, and unauthorized file-sharing access | MIT-013 |

---

## 5. Risk Assessment (OCTAVE / STRIDE)

| Asset | Threat | Impact | Likelihood | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ASSET-001 | Legacy and vulnerable service exposure | Credential theft, unauthorized remote access, lateral movement, and host compromise | High | Critical |
| ASSET-001 | Exposed database services | Unauthorized database access, data exfiltration, and integrity compromise | High | High |
| ASSET-001 | Exposed file-sharing and remote administration services | Unauthorized file access, remote command execution, and privilege escalation | High | Critical |
| ASSET-001 | Exposed web and middleware services | Web application compromise, backend service abuse, and remote exploitation | Medium | High |
| ASSET-002 | Exposed SSH and HTTP services | Brute-force attempts, web service exploitation, and unauthorized access | Medium | Medium |
| ASSET-003 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB services | SMB enumeration, credential relay, unauthorized file-sharing access, and lateral movement | High | High |

---

## 6. Mitigation Recommendations

### Mitigation Recommendations

| MIT ID | Vulnerability | Recommended Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| MIT-001 | Exposed vsftpd 2.3.4 FTP Service | Disable vulnerable FTP service or replace it with SFTP/FTPS and upgrade to a supported secure version |
| MIT-002 | Exposed Telnet Service | Disable Telnet and use SSH with strong authentication and restricted network access |
| MIT-003 | Outdated OpenSSH Service | Upgrade OpenSSH, disable weak algorithms, enforce key-based authentication, and restrict administrative access |
| MIT-004 | Exposed Web and AJP Services | Patch Apache and Tomcat components, restrict AJP to trusted internal hosts, and disable unused web services |
| MIT-005 | Exposed Samba NetBIOS Services | Restrict SMB and NetBIOS to trusted networks, disable anonymous access, and harden share permissions |
| MIT-006 | Exposed r-services | Disable exec and login services and replace them with hardened SSH-based administration |
| MIT-007 | Exposed Bindshell Service | Remove the bindshell immediately, isolate the host, and investigate for compromise |
| MIT-008 | Exposed NFS and rpcbind Services | Restrict NFS and rpcbind to approved hosts, enforce least-privilege exports, and block unnecessary access |
| MIT-009 | Exposed Database Services | Bind databases to trusted interfaces only, enforce strong authentication, patch database versions, and apply least privilege |
| MIT-010 | Exposed VNC and X11 Services | Restrict VNC and X11 to trusted networks or encrypted tunnels and enforce strong authentication |
| MIT-011 | Exposed Java RMI and IRC Services | Restrict Java RMI and IRC services, patch or remove unused components, and monitor for abuse |
| MIT-012 | Exposed SSH and HTTP Services | Harden SSH and HTTP services with patching, secure configuration, access control, and monitoring |
| MIT-013 | Exposed Windows RPC and SMB Services | Restrict RPC and SMB to trusted networks, enable SMB signing, patch Windows services, and disable unnecessary file-sharing exposure |

---

## 7. Conclusion

The assessed environment demonstrates a security posture that requires prioritised remediation, particularly around the concentration of critical and high-risk exposures on 192.168.56.20. The presence of legacy protocols, exposed administrative services, database access, file-sharing services, and shell-like access pathways substantially increases the likelihood of unauthorised access, credential compromise, lateral movement, and service abuse. Remediation should therefore focus first on removing unnecessary and high-risk services, restricting access to trusted networks, strengthening authentication, patching outdated components, and improving monitoring. Addressing these areas will reduce the attack surface and improve the resilience of the assessed environment.
